postText: This is one of the most fascinating and under-appreciated figures of the recent IPCC AR6 report (Figure 6.16). It shows the effects of a single year of emissions after 10 and 100 years, and really illustrates the difference between stock and flow pollutants: www.ipcc.ch/report/a...
bundle.chartTitle: Effect of a one year pulse of present-day emissions on global surface temperature
keyFindings: Methane (CH4) is a dominant contributor to short-term (10-year) warming, particularly from agriculture and fossil fuel production and distribution sectors.
Sulfur dioxide (SO2) emissions, primarily from fossil fuel combustion for energy and industry, provide a significant cooling effect in the short term (10 years) that largely dissipates by 100 years.
Carbon dioxide (CO2) is the primary driver of long-term (100-year) warming across nearly all sectors and regions.
Eastern Asia has the largest regional impact on GSAT, driven by a high volume of both warming and cooling emissions.
The shipping sector shows a net cooling effect after 10 years due to high sulfur emissions, which offsets the warming from its CO2 and other greenhouse gases.
